betterquesting:10.desc:8:
§6Steam§f

§aThe lifeblood of nuclear power — understand steam densities and turbine conversion!§f

§bSteam§f is a gas produced by boiling water using heat from reactors or boilers. It is the primary medium for converting nuclear heat into electrical power.

§6Production§f: When §e1mB§f of water receives §e200TU§f of heat, it becomes §e100mB§f of Steam (100°C).

§6Steam Densities§f (each stage is 10× denser than the last):
§b• Steam§f (100°C) — 2 TU/10mB; base form
§b• Dense Steam§f (300°C) — 18 TU/10mB; 10× less volume
§b• Super Dense Steam§f (450°C) — 120 TU/10mB; output by ZIRNOX and Fusion boiler
§b• Ultra Dense Steam§f (600°C) — 120 TU/1mB; densest form
§b• Low-Pressure Steam§f (~20°C) — no power; output from turbines

§6Turbine Conversion§f: Feeding steam through a turbine steps it down one density level, producing power and increasing volume by 10×. Ultra Dense → Super Dense → Dense → Steam → Low-Pressure Steam. The final stage must be condensed back to water in a cooling tower or steam condenser.

§6RBMK Bonus§f: Higher density steam in the RBMK produces up to §e25%§f more power at Ultra Dense.

§6Other Uses§f: Steam is vital for §bhydrocarbon cracking§f in oil processing.

§eTip§f: A ZIRNOX requires at least §e3§f steam turbines and a condenser to form a closed cooling loop. Plan your turbine setup to match your reactor's steam output!
